coredata.dc:title: Optically active polymer nanocomposite composed of polyaniline, polyacrylonitrile and green-synthesized graphene quantum dot for supercapacitor application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Arthisree, D.
Madhuri, W.
coredata.dc:description: Development of stable and optically active composite film that can show efficient supercapacitor activity is a challenging research interest in material science. A polymer nanocomposite film composed of a green synthesised graphene quantum dot (GQD, G) doped polyacrylonitrile (PAN) and polyaniline (PANI) systems, designated as PAN-PANI@G, have been prepared. The critical loading concentration of prepared composite and its effect on the physicochemical, optical and electrical analysis were systematically studied in detail. PAN-PANI@G composite showed an appreciable electrical conductivity (2.362 × 10−6 S m−1) and optical absorbance at λ max ~270 nm. Physicochemical characterizations by XRD, FTIR and TEM analysis reveal chemical interaction between the individual components via intermolecular hydrogen bonding. Analysed particle size of GQD in the polymer membrane was found to be about two times lesser than that of the pure GQD highlighting synergic interaction between the individual GQD and PAN/PANI matrix. A specific capacitance value of the polymer composites that have been modified on screen-printed carbon electrode were tested by cyclic voltammetric and galvanostatic charge-discharge techniques in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution. Calculated supercapacitor values at an applied current density, 670 mA g−1 are in a range of 105–587 F g−1 cm−2 which are approximately 2–1300 times higher than the values reported for polypyrrole and polyaniline based polymer composite film in the literature. As a preliminary extension of this study, the optimal PAN/PANI@G nanocomposite of GQD loading, 1.5 wt% was extended to prototype supercapacitor cell application in combination with a dilute solution of NaCl along with suitable conducting plates. When 3 V DC power was supplied for 4 min, the prototype cell produced an operating voltage of 1.4 V for 1 h of operating time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Polyacrylonitrile-polyaniline composite
I–V analysis
Synergic interaction
Supercapacitor
Prototype cell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of PAN/PANI@G composites and its modified electrodes

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to Dr. Annamalai Senthil Kumar for supporting and providing valuable suggestions on electrochemical studies. Authors greatly acknowledge the facilities provided by Dr. S. Kaleemulla for I–V characteristics. We acknowledge VIT, Vellore for providing TEM facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction pattern of various polymer nanocomposite systems a. PAN/PANI blend, b. PAN/PANI@G-0.5 wt%, c. PAN/PANI@G-1wt% and d. PAN/PANI@G-1.5 wt%.
FTIR spectrum of various polymer nanocomposite systems; a. PAN/PANI blend, b. PAN/PANI@G-0.5 wt%, c. PAN/PANI@G-1wt%, d. PAN/PANI@G-1.5 wt%.
UV–Vis spectrum of various polymer nanocomposite systems dissolved in DMF solvent; A. PANI, B. (a–c) PAN, GQD, PAN/PANI blend, C (a–c) PAN/PANI@G-0.5 wt%, PAN/PANI@G-1 wt%, PAN/PANI@G-1.5 wt%.
TEM images of A. Pure GQD, B-D. PAN/PANI@G-1.5 wt% composite at different magnifications. G-GQD.
I–V characteristics of various polymer nanocomposite systems a. PAN/PANI blend, b. PAN/PANI@G-0.5 wt%, c. PAN-PANI@G-1wt%, d. PAN-PANI@G-1.5 wt%.

A. CV response of a. GQD, b. PAN-PANI blend, B. (a–c) CV response of PAN/PANI@G-0.5 wt%, PAN/PANI@G-1 wt%, PAN/PANI@G-1.5 wt% composites, C. (a–d) Galvanostatic charge/discharge response of PAN/PANI blend, PAN/PANI@G-0.5 wt%, PAN/PANI@G-1 wt%, PAN/PANI@G-1.5 wt% composites at a current density of 670 mA g−1 in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution.
Illustration of PAN/PANI@G nanocomposite and its proton-coupled electron-transfer reaction responsible for the pseudocapacitance activity. G-GQD.
(A) Illustration for PAN/PANI@G-1.5 wt% based capacitor and (B) its typical digital photograph of optimal composite verified for voltage generation.
XRD details of various composite materials prepared in this work.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: G0.5 wt%- GQD of 0.5 wt %; G1 wt%- GQD of 1 wt %; G1.5 wt%- GQD of 1.5 wt %; PAN-polyacrylonitrile; PANI-polyaniline; G-GQD.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Literature reports on polymer film composites for supercapacitors.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: HPC- hierarchically porous carbon; PANI-polyaniline; rGO-reduced graphene oxide; PPy-polypyrrole; BC- Bacterial cellulose paper; GNF-graphene Nano flake; f-CNT-functionalized carbon nanotube; C- cellulose; PAN-polyacrylonitrile; GQD-graphene quantum dot (G).

head.ce:title: Optically active polymer nanocomposite composed of polyaniline, polyacrylonitrile and green-synthesized graphene quantum dot for supercapacitor application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ceramic Composite Laboratory, Centre for Crystal Growth, Department of Physics, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, 632014, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ceramic Composite Laboratory, Centre for Crystal Growth, Department of Physics, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, 632014, Tamilnadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of stable and optically active composite film that can show efficient supercapacitor activity is a challenging research interest in material science. A polymer nanocomposite film composed of a green synthesised graphene quantum dot (GQD, G) doped polyacrylonitrile (PAN) and polyaniline (PANI) systems, designated as PAN-PANI@G, have been prepared. The critical loading concentration of prepared composite and its effect on the physicochemical, optical and electrical analysis were systematically studied in detail. PAN-PANI@G composite showed an appreciable electrical conductivity (2.362 × 10−6 S m−1) and optical absorbance at λ max ~270 nm. Physicochemical characterizations by XRD, FTIR and TEM analysis reveal chemical interaction between the individual components via intermolecular hydrogen bonding. Analysed particle size of GQD in the polymer membrane was found to be about two times lesser than that of the pure GQD highlighting synergic interaction between the individual GQD and PAN/PANI matrix. A specific capacitance value of the polymer composites that have been modified on screen-printed carbon electrode were tested by cyclic voltammetric and galvanostatic charge-discharge techniques in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution. Calculated supercapacitor values at an applied current density, 670 mA g−1 are in a range of 105–587 F g−1 cm−2 which are approximately 2–1300 times higher than the values reported for polypyrrole and polyaniline based polymer composite film in the literature. As a preliminary extension of this study, the optimal PAN/PANI@G nanocomposite of GQD loading, 1.5 wt% was extended to prototype supercapacitor cell application in combination with a dilute solution of NaCl along with suitable conducting plates. When 3 V DC power was supplied for 4 min, the prototype cell produced an operating voltage of 1.4 V for 1 h of operating time.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent days highly efficient, eco-friendly, cost-effective energy storage device that can show multiple physical properties like conductivity, optical property, mechanical stability and surface defect free films is a demanding research interest [1]. Supercapacitor based electrochemical energy storage system is considered as one of the most promising and well-suited techniques at the utility level [2–4]. In general, supercapacitors have high power density over the ordinary capacitors. Often it is preferred more than ordinary battery owing to its fast, simple charging and faster delivery of charge [5]. For instance, supercapacitor based hybrid vehicles and supplementary storage battery for electric vehicle systems. The functioning of supercapacitors is determined by various factors like dopant used, surface area, conductivity, porosity and other surface-related properties of the preferred materials [6]. Based on the mechanism, they are categorised as follows: Electrical double-layer capacitor (EDLC) and pseudocapacitance response (fast faradaic redox reaction). Usually, the carbon based material possesses EDLC mechanism, whereas conducting polymers and metal oxide systems have pseudo-capacitance behaviour [7]. In a view of combining the EDCL and pseudocapacitance performances and to elevate the capacitance property, nanocarbon/conducting polymer composite has been introduced [8–11]. Meanwhile, carbon based materials doped with polymers have been reported to enhance the electrical conductivity, mechanical stability, optical property, thereby yielding high-performance hybrid composites for various engineering applications [12–14]. Indeed, tuning the carbon materials based supercapacitor as conducting and optically active film is a challenging task. It is noteworthy that although carbon and certain conducting polymers like polyaniline (PANI) [15], poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate [16] and polypyrrole [17] can provide good electrical conductivity and high supercapacitor activity, it is not suitable for mechanically stable film formation [18,19]. For example, Wu et al. reported PANI/graphene based composite for efficient supercapacitor activity (201 F g−1), but failed to provide a stable film formation [18]. On the other hand, polymer like polyacrylonitrile (PAN), Polymethyl methacrylate, Polyvinylidene fluoride and epoxy can yield dimensionally stable film formation, but its conductivity and supercapacitor activities are almost nil [20]. In fact to retain the stability and optical property in polymer composite system, one has to partially sacrifice the conductivity and/or supercapacitor activity. To the best of our knowledge, only one optically active polymer composite composed of Polypyrrole - poly[1-[4-(3-carboxy-4-hydroxyphenylazo)benzenesulfonamido]-1,2-ethanediyl, sodium salt] (PAZO)-MWCNT, has been reported for superconductivity activity (200 Fg-1) [21]. The reported composite was prepared using expensive precursor materials like pyrrole, MWCNT, benzenesulfonamido derivatives. Herein, we report an optically active and mechanically stable polymer nanocomposite that is composed of low cost materials like polyacrylonitrile (PAN), polyaniline (PANI) and a green synthesised graphene quantum dots (GQD,G) (bottom-up approach based on carbohydrate based precursor molecules) for efficient supercapacitor application.

GQD, a few (2–50 nm) nanometer sized particles, is a newly emerging, well-known, highly efficient, low toxic, graphene based nanomaterial that possess superior electrical [22], optical properties [23] and hence it has been utilized in many energy harvesting [24] and storage kind of applications [25]. Bottom-up synthesising of GQD from an alternative source of carbon like glucose powder [26], citric acid [27], corn powder [28], rise husk [29] is considered as an environmentally friendly and facile method, which produces more homogeneous, less defect and uniform particle size than that of top-down approach [30]. Meanwhile, PAN is an organic polymer resin with good thermal stability, chemical stability and high tensile strength property [31]. It acts as a good binder for blending with other polymers and easy fabrication of nanomaterials [32]. There are few PAN/PANI blends have been reported for multifunctional applications like conductivity [33], antibacterial activity [34], filtration membrane [35] etc. A PAN/PANI@G polymer composite prepared in this work showed an enhanced specific capacitance value at low applied current in comparison with other reported polymer composite films [36–40]. Scope and objective of this work is to develop optically active-multifunctional polymer composite film for supercapacitor application.

Fibre yarn of polyacrylonitrile polymer (count of yarn 4/16) was obtained from Piyush Syndicate, Mumbai, India. Polyaniline (emeraldine salt) average MW > 15,000 powder, dimethylformamide (DMF), concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich USA and other chemicals are used in analytical grade as received.

GQD was synthesised via hydrothermal autoclave method from rice powder as per our previous published paper [41]. Shortly, 2 g of rice powder was dispersed in 40 ml DD-water and autoclaved at 150 οC for 6 h. The final product was filtered using slide-A-Lyzer kit: 10 kda MWCO spin filtering membrane, 3–12 ml capacity for 24 h followed by centrifuging at 5000 rpm for 1 h. The resultant GQD solution was subjected to fluorescence and TEM analysis to quantify the particle size and fluorescence property.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PAN/PANI@G composites and its modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: In first, following stock solutions were prepared; 1 g PAN dissolved in 50 ml DMF, 0.5 g PANI and 0.5 ml GQD are dispersed in 5 ml DMF separately. Furthermore, four different concentrations of PAN/PANI@G (PAN: PANI: GQD = 9:1:0, 9:0.5:0.5, 8:1:1, 7:1.5:1.5) were prepared by solution phase mixing method, where in, dilute solutions of the desired polymers were mixed deliberately and magnetically stirred for 12 h at room temperature. For film formation, 10 ml of the “as prepared composite solution” precursors were spread uniformly on to a clean glass petri dishes (10 cm diameter) by drop-casting method and dried at room temperature for about 76 h. According to the weight ratios, the as-prepared composites were named as PAN/PANI@G-0.5 wt%, PAN/PANI@G- 1 wt%, PAN/PANI@G-1.5 wt% and compared with PAN/PANI blend (9:1) and pure GQD. Illustration of the prepared composite is depicted in Scheme 1 . For electrochemical studies, 6 ml of the polymer composite solution, which is equal to 100 mg ml−1 of active polymer composite material, was drop-casted on 3 mm diameter screen-printed carbon electrode and dried in room temperature for 2 h. For (I–V) measurements, two probe electrical measurement systems was used directly on the as prepared film.

The fluorescence activity was measured with bright green-illumination qualitatively by exposing the GQD under UV chamber (Lablink Instrument, Hyderabad, India). X-ray diffraction experiment was carried out using Bruker AXS D8 focus advanced X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan, Tokyo) instrument. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured using Shimazdu, IR affinity −1 spectrometer. Ultraviolet and visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum has been recorded with Shimazdu UV-2401PC spectrometer. A Techni G2–20 TEM instrument was used to examine the microstructure and particle size of the prepared composite. The current-voltage (I–V) measurements were recorded using Keithley's next-generation Source Meter (SMU) instrument model number 2450. All electrochemical experiments were carried out by a CHI model 440E electrochemical workstation (USA) using a three-electrode system consisting of screen printed carbon electrode (3 mm diameter size) and its chemically modified active polymer composite material as a working electrode, Ag/AgCl as a reference and platinum wire as the auxiliary electrode with 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution was used as electrolyte. The cyclic voltammetry curves were measured at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 and galvanostatic charge/discharge curves were measured at a current density value of 0.67 Ag-1.

The XRD pattern of the pure blend (PAN/PANI) and GQD doped nanocomposite (PAN/PANI@G-0.5–1.5 wt%) is shown in Fig. 1 (a–d). The diffraction pattern of all the samples exhibits two prominent broad amorphous peaks at 2θ = 16.77ο and 29.39ο which refers to the hexagonal structure of the PAN [40] and the pseudo-orthorhombic phase of amorphous PANI polymer matrix [42]. It can be clearly seen that the intensity of the first peak greatly reduces as the loading concentration of GQD increases in the polymer composite. The crystallite size of the particles in the film was estimated using the Scherrer's formula [43] and shown in Table 1 respectively. As can be seen from the table, the crystallite size increases (4.15–5.06 nm) with an increase in GQD concentration up to 1 wt%. But for the highest loading of GQD 1.5 wt% (Fig. 1d), a low crystallite size (3.74 nm) was obtained. This observation is due to adsorption and intercalation of GQD on the surface of polymer blend [44]. Therefore to further understand the chemical interactions of polymer blend with GQD, the nanocomposite films are characterised using FTIR.

Fig. 2 presents the FTIR spectra of PAN/PANI blend and various wt% loading of GQD in the (0.5–1.5 wt%) polymer composite system. The typical spectrum of polymer blend (Fig. 2a) is in good agreement with previous literature reports on PAN/PANI nanocomposites [45,46]. Following, there are some of the IR signals of various functional groups of PAN/PANI blend, N–H stretching at 3452 cm−1, CHCH stretching at 2922 cm−1, C≡N stretching at 2241 cm−1, adsorbed CO2 at 1734 cm−1 [47], CC stretching at 1660 cm−1, N–H bending at 1581 cm−1, CH2 bending at 1450-1371 cm−1, C–N stretching at 1294-1230 cm−1 of the benzene and quinoidal ring of PANI, N–H stretching at cm−1, C–N stretching at 1029 cm−1, CC bending at 812-678 cm−1. Furthermore, Fig. 2b–d represents the FTIR spectrum of various wt% loading of GQD (from 0.5 to 1.5 wt%) in the polymer system. The composites show qualitatively similar IR peak signals with slight variation (±3 cm−1) in the intensity and frequency region. Interestingly, for the higher loading of GQD (1.5 wt%) composite when compared with PAN/PANI blend (Fig. 2d), new peaks emerge at 3500, 2854 and 1163 cm−1 corresponding to –OH, CHCH and C–O stretching vibrations [48]. This observation may be due to the presence of oxygen and aromatic carbon functional groups in the graphitic fine structure of GQD and its strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding with other functional groups (-COOH, –OH, N–H and C≡N) of polymer blends [49]. To confirm the electronic transition mechanism of the prepared system, UV–Vis spectroscopy was carried out.

The UV–Vis spectra of PANI, PAN, GQD and PAN/PANI blend are depicted in Fig. 3 A, B (a-c). The absorbance of PANI showed a peak at 272 nm corresponding to the π-π* transition of conjugation length [50] and a shoulder peak at 468 nm due to charged cationic species assigned to the polar bond transition of PANI (emeraldine salt) [51,52]. As can be seen from Fig. 3B (a-c) the PAN exhibit a prominent peak at 273 nm due to the n-π* transition corresponding to the nitrile group present in the polymer [53]. Meanwhile, GQD shows a well-defined and intense UV absorbance at 284 nm due to n-π* transition of CO bonding [54]. Whereas the PAN/PANI blend showed a peak at 273 nm relating to the combination effect of individual polymers. Loading different wt% of GQD with the polymer system influence on absorbance trend of the composite system is shown in Fig. 3C (a-c). As the loading wt% of GQD in the composite increase, the absorbance at λ = 273 nm tends to decrease considerably indicating the π-stacking like arrangement of GQD on PANI in the polymer matrix. Introducing carbonaceous material in the polymer composite can considerably downplay the absorbance magnitude supporting the π conjugation existence in the matrix [55]. Similar kind of observation was noticed by Luk et al. and In het Panhuis et al. for PANI-GQD and PANI-MWCNT composite systems [56,57]. It is likely that the particle size of GQD has an important role in altering the absorbance trend of the composite system [58]. Overall, it is obvious that the polymer nanocomposite has significant tunable optical activity for multifunctional application. To identify the particle size and their distribution in the polymer system, the higher loading composite (PAN/PANI@G-1.5 wt%) was subjected to TEM analysis.

Fig. 4A shows TEM image of GQD. The as-prepared GQD showed particle distribution in the range of 45–50 nm. Furthermore, Fig. 4 B–D depicts the TEM images of PAN/PANI@G-1.5 wt%, in which a well dispersed and adsorbed GQD in the polymer blend, was clearly seen without any apparent agglomerations. Fig. 4D is the enlarged view of the higher loading polymer composite system, showing a circular dot like structure of particle size 25–50 nm, which indicates the embedded GQD's on the polymer matrix. Moreover, the particle size in the polymer composites is lesser than the pure GQD, which may be due to the mobilization of part of the monolayers of GQD in the polymer system.

In order to study the electrical property of pure polymer blend and GQD loaded nanocomposite, we performed the current versus voltage characteristic for all the samples and their graphical trends are depicted in Fig. 5 a–d. This study was performed with an applied potential ranging from 0 to +5 V at room temperature. As can be seen from Fig. 5a–d, the pure blend and 0.5 wt% loaded nanocomposite are found to exhibit feeble electrical conductivity around 1.090 × 10−7 S m−1. Interestingly, for the higher loading GQD composites (1–1.5 wt%) the conductivity was gradually increased to 1.323 × 10−6 S m−1 and 2.362 × 10−6 S m−1 respectively. Such enhancement in electrical conductivity for the higher loaded nanocomposite may be due to the existence of high carrier density and electron mobility of GQD on the active surface area of PAN/PANI membrane [59]. It is noteworthy, that the conductivity value of present PAN/PANI@G composites are comparable with the reported conductivity values of PANI system (emeraldine salt) between 10−8 to 101 S m−1, owing to the different structural forms like emeraldine base (nearly non-conducting system), emeraldine salt, pernigraniline, leucoemeraldine (fully reduced form) used while synthesising PANI polymer [15].

To study the electrochemical characteristics of prepared composites, the CV analysis was performed for the active material (prepared composite) modified screen-printed carbon electrode in 0.1 M H2SO4 electrolyte, operated with the potential window ranging from 0 to 0.8 V vs Ag/AgCl at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. 6 A (a, b) the difference in the current signals of the individual GQD and PAN/PANI blend systems are easily distinguishable. Being a carbonaceous material, GQD possesses an excellent electrical double-layer capacitor (EDLC) [60] property which is clearly identified from its CV response. Moreover, the CV curve of PAN/PANI blend has shown a large conducting area than that of pure GQD. This indicates that PAN/PANI blend exhibit higher capacitance property due to high surface area and pseudocapacitance nature of PANI polymer [17,61]. Therefore, the modified composite of PAN/PANI with various loading wt% of GQD deposited on the electrode may be effective in enhancing the capacitance performance. Fig. 6B (a–c) represents the CV of the PAN/PANI@G (0.5–1.5 wt%) composites recorded at scan rate, 50 mV s−1. It was noted that the area of cathodic and anodic portions increase significantly with an increase in loading concentration of GQD (0.5–1.5 wt%), which is accompanied by an appreciate rectangular shape along with feeble redox peak suggesting the pseudocapacitance mechanism of the composite material. Marked deviation in the voltammetric pattern noticed at high loading (Fig. 6B, curve c) is due to over-lapping of the Faradaic and non-Faradic systems along with limitation in the mass-transfer reaction. The pseudo-capacitive behaviour of the PAN/PANI blend is merely due to proton-coupled electron-transfers of the PANI and GQD active sites on the matrix. Indeed, GQD contribution to the overall pseudocapacitance is very little. The PANI electrochemical reaction is well-established in the literature [62]. The possible mechanistic feature of the reaction is sketched in Scheme 1.

Interestingly, the highest loading concentration of GQD (1.5 wt%) was found to exhibit large capacitive current around 7.35 mA, which is three times higher than that of the composites with less concentration of GQD (0.5 and 1 wt% loading). This enhanced current signal and pseudocapacitance behaviour of the prepared composite may be due to the loading effect of polymer blend/GQD and chemical interaction between the functional groups (especially –COOH, –OH) of GQD and PAN/PANI membrane (-C≡N, N–H), which are highly evident from our results.

In order to further investigate the capacitance property and quantitively estimate the capacitance, the prepared polymer blend and composites as a modified screen-printed electrode were subjected to galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) test. Fig. 6C (a–d) shows the charge/discharge curve of PAN/PANI blend and PAN/PANI@G (0.5–1.5 wt%) composites at a fixed current density of 0.67 Ag-1. It is noted that all the samples exhibit a triangle shape, suggesting that the charge storage potential linearly increases with respect to time and indicates stable reversibility during discharging. To verify the individual capacitance value of samples, the specific capacitance (C s) of the electrode is calculated using the following equation [63]:
(1)
C
s
=
I × d t
m × Δ V
where, C
s is the specific capacitance (F g−1), I is the input current (A), dt is discharge time (s), ΔV is potential change during discharge process (V) and m is the mass of active material deposited on the electrode (g). After substituting the values, the GCD specific capacitance measurements were derived to be 8.840 Fg−1cm−2 (for PAN/PANI blend), 105.25 Fg−1cm−2 (for PAN/PANI@G-0.5 wt%), 144.97 Fg−1cm−2 (for PAN/PANI@G-1 wt%), 589.34 Fg−1cm−2 (for PAN/PANI@G-1.5 wt%), respectively. Table 2 summarizes the capacitance data of the present system in comparison with some literature reports based on polymer composite films of PANI/hierarchically porous carbon (290 Fg−1cm−2) [36], polypyrrole (PPy)/reduced graphene oxide (rGO)/Bacterial cellulose paper (190 Fg−1cm−2) [37], rGO/PPy (0.44 Fg−1cm−2) [38], graphene nano flake/PPy (49.3 Fg−1cm−2) [39], PPy/functionalized carbon nanotube (f-CNT)/cellulose (214 Fg−1cm−2) [40]. It is noteworthy that in the present system C s value of composite with higher concentration of GQD (1.5 wt%) was obviously enhanced in comparison with the polymer blend and other composites. The large capacitance was mainly due to the synergic functional group interaction between the GQD/polymer membrane [64–66] and large surface area of PANI polymer chain. We have tabulated various capacitance value reported from the literatures on polymer/carbon based composite films in Table 2. Hence we conclude that, in comparison with these literature reports [34–38], the GQD based polymer nanocomposite film reported in the present work exhibits (589.2 Fg−1cm−2) about 2–1330 times higher capacitance value than the polymer composite films reported in the literature (Table 2). To further confirm the potential application of the optimal composite in the electronic and portable device, a prototype supercapacitor system composited of PAN/PANI@G-1.5 wt% was developed by sandwiching 5 ml of the composite solution between 0.1 wt% NaCl solution immersed laboratory filter paper and Aluminium foil as shown in Scheme 2 . Prior to the supercapacitor experiment, the prototype cell was charged at an applied voltage, 3 V for 4 min. Note that the water decomposition reaction, 2H2O→O2+4H++4e− (Eo = 1.46 V vs Ag/AgCl in pH 1), is favourable at the high positive potential, 3 V. Indeed, very feeble O2 gas evolution reaction on the electrode surface was noticed, which may be due to the high over-potential. In fact, such a feeble gas evolution reaction open-up certain blacked pores and active-site of the composite system which in turn to enhanced pseudocapacitance activity. Typical photograph of the charged supercapacitor was shown in Scheme 2 displaying 1.4 ± 0.1 V of output power up to 60 min of working time. This observation indicates appreciable supercapacitor performance of the PAN/PANI@G-1.5 wt% system that can be extendable to simple and low cost device fabrication, power-saving supercapacitor/storage kind of applications. As an independent experiment, the composite modified electrode was potential cycled for 20 times at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 in the test electrolyte. There is no marked alteration in the anodic and cathodic current signal were noticed highlighting the good stability of the composite system. Indeed, additional experiments were needed to extend the composite into proto-type device.

We have successfully prepared a new polymer nanocomposite by doping various loading concentration of a green synthesised GQD into PAN/PANI polymer matrix. As a result of inclusion of GQD in the polymer system, changes in the crystallite size and peak intensity for the pure and GQD loaded composite were noticed from XRD studies. Strong chemical interaction and intermolecular hydrogen bonding between the functional group of the components lead to generation of new peaks at 3500, 2854 and 1163 cm−1 from FTIR spectroscopy experiment. Both polymer blend and GQD are found to exhibit their combination effect on electronic transitions of the nanocomposite system. The suppression in the absorbance trend as a function of loading nanoparticle signifies the tunable optical activity of the composite system, as illustrated from the UV–Vis results. The particle size and distribution of GQD in the polymer matrix is noticed in the TEM morphology. The I–V analysis demonstrated a gradual increase in the electrical conductivity (1.090 × 10−7 S m−1 to 2.362 × 10−6 S m−1) as a function of loading concentration of GQD in the polymer matrix with an applied voltage from 0 to 5 V. The enhancement in the supercapacitance property was studied by electrochemical CV and GCD analysis. The maximum specific capacitance value was reached up to 589.2 Fg−1cm−2 at 0.67 Ag-1 for PAN/PANI@G-1.5 wt% composites system. This optimal composite was designed as a prototype cell and tested for its practical application in electronic and portable devices. Hence this optimal composite can further be developed as a simple power-saving supercapacitor kind of application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Dr. Annamalai Senthil Kumar for supporting and providing valuable suggestions on electrochemical studies. Authors greatly acknowledge the facilities provided by Dr. S. Kaleemulla for I–V characteristics. We acknowledge VIT, Vellore for providing TEM facility.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Co-benefit analysis of incentives for energy generation and storage systems; a multi-stakeholder perspective

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Haghi, F. Michael Fowler, R. Kaamran, Co-benefit analysis of incentives for energy generation and storage systems; a multi-stakeholder perspective, Int J Hydrogen Energy 44 (2019) 9643-9671.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A high dielectric composite for energy storage application
